content.text:
**Disagree.**

The policy covers forced-entry damage only when responders enter **because of an actual emergency or a perceived emergency involving Joanne**. Here, Joanne **knew** the crashing sounds were just her dogs playing and called police to help subdue them, so there was no genuine or reasonably perceived emergency involving her that triggered coverage under the definition.
Agree.

Agree.

The policy covers damage caused when first responders force entry due to an “emergency or perceived emergency” involving the policyholder. Joanne reported what she believed was a burglary (a perceived emergency), and the police forced entry in response to that perceived emergency. The fact that it turned out to be a false alarm does not remove it from the policy’s “perceived emergency” coverage as written.

**Disagree.**  

The policy covers damage caused when **first responders** force entry due to an emergency involving Salma. Here, the forced entry was made by **Sam (a friend/accountant), not a first responder**, so the damage does not fall within the policy’s defined “Emergency Damages” coverage.  

**Final answer: Disagree.**

Disagree.

The policy covers **loss or damage to the contents** of Salma’s home caused by forced entry during an emergency. The forced-entry damage here is most naturally to the **home/structure (e.g., door, lock, frame)**, which is **not “contents.”** Even though there was an emergency involving Salma, the claimed damage doesn’t fall within the defined coverage.

**Disagree.**

Even though there was a real emergency (a burglar) involving Salma, the policy only covers damage caused when **“first responders”** force entry. Sam was an **off-duty** firefighter acting as a **private individual/friend**, not responding in an official first-responder capacity. Therefore, the forced-entry damage would likely **not** fall within the policy’s definition of “Emergency Damages.”

**Final answer: Disagree.**

Disagree.

The policy covers “oil leaking from a fixed oil-fired heating installation.” Madison’s damage came from spilling kerosene she was carrying, not from a leak in a fixed heating installation, so it doesn’t fit the defined “Escape of Oil” coverage.
**Agree.** The oil is leaking from a **fixed oil-fired heating installation** (her heater), which fits the policy’s definition of “Escape of Oil,” so the resulting floor damage is covered.

**Agree.** The damage was caused by oil escaping/leaking from a **fixed oil‑fired heating installation**, which fits the policy’s definition of “Escape of Oil,” even if the escape was triggered by the friend’s mistake during a repair attempt.

Disagree.

The policy covers oil leaking from a **fixed oil‑fired heating installation**. A **portable oil lamp** is not a fixed heating installation, so the damage would not fall within the “Escape of Oil” definition.
Agree. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Disagree.** The policy covers oil leaking from a **fixed oil‑fired heating installation**, and a wall‑mounted **oil lamp** is not a heating installation, so the damage would not fall within the “Escape of Oil” definition.
Disagree.
Agree.

Agree

The damage was caused by an unforeseen flow of water from an internal source (a leaking pipe), and it occurred over a short period (several hours), which fits the “sudden, unforeseen” Escape of Water definition.

**Agree.**

The leaking goldfish bowl is an **internal source** of water within the home, and the leak was **unexpected**, causing water to escape and damage the building—fitting the policy definition of “Escape of Water” as provided.
Agree.

**Agree.** The leak from the water cooler is an unexpected escape of water from an internal source (a household appliance/container), resulting in damage to the building, which fits the policy’s “Escape of Water” definition.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage caused by a sudden, unforeseen escape/flow of **water** from an internal source. Here, the substance that escaped was **paint**, not water, so it doesn’t fit the policy’s “Escape of Water” definition as stated.
Agree

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage caused by a sudden, unforeseen flow of **water** from an internal source (e.g., burst pipe). Here, the escaping liquid was **tomato juice**, not water, so it doesn’t fall within the stated “Escape of Water” definition even though the tub crack was sudden and unforeseen.

Disagree.

The policy only covers “Fire” damage caused by a **naturally-occurring fire**. Here, the fire was triggered by a **defective propane grill/tank explosion** (a non-natural, man-made cause), so it doesn’t fit the policy’s definition of covered “Fire.”

**Agree.** The damage to Fatema’s garage was caused by a brush fire that spread onto her property—i.e., a **naturally occurring fire** as described—before the grill’s propane explosion occurred. Therefore, the garage damage falls within the policy’s “Fire” coverage. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage “caused by naturally-occurring fire.” Here, the garage burned because the **propane tank exploded and ignited the garage**—a fire resulting from a **man-made fuel source (the grill/propane)** rather than the naturally occurring brush fire itself. The natural brush fire embers were a trigger, but the damage was caused by a different (non‑natural) fire.

Disagree.

The policy defines “Flooding” as water invading the property due to a sudden release **from outside the buildings**. Here, the water came from a leaking **waterbed inside** the home, so it does not meet the policy’s definition of covered “Flooding.”

Agree.

The damage fits the policy’s definition of “Flooding” because a large volume of water suddenly released from outside the building (the broken fire hydrant) invaded the property and caused the damage. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Agree.** The water damage resulted from **a large volume of water** that **suddenly released from outside the building** (the leaking rain barrel next to the home) and **invaded the property**, fitting the policy’s definition of “Flooding.”
Disagree.

Agree

Final answer is: **Agree**
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**

Disagree.

The policy covers **damage to the plants themselves** caused by strikes (or similar disturbances). Here, the strike affected the **hardware store’s operations**, and the plants died from **lack of fertilizer** due to Genie’s choice not to buy it elsewhere—an indirect/consequential loss rather than damage caused by the strike within the insured property.

**Agree.** The plants were damaged within the property boundaries and the damage was caused by activities arising out of a **strike** (the picketing spilling onto Genie's land), which fits the policy’s definition of covered “Garden Plants” damage.

Disagree.

The Garden Plants coverage is for **direct damage to the plants caused by** events like **strikes or civil commotion**. Here, the strike caused a **fertilizer shortage**, and the plants died from **lack of fertilizer**, not from any **direct physical damage** resulting from the strike itself. This is an indirect/consequential loss, so it would not fall within the stated coverage.
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Agree

The damage was to garden plants on Margot’s property and was caused by masked individuals deliberately trampling them, which fits “malicious people or vandals” under the Garden Plants coverage definition.
Disagree
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Agree.** Gavin’s injury happened while performing his normal job duties under regular working conditions (accessing a second-story A/C unit via a ladder), and the injury caused missed employment, so it falls within the stated “General Damages” coverage.

**Disagree.**

Gavin’s injury happened while helping his girlfriend at her home, not **“under regular working conditions”** as part of his employment. Even though he was doing the same type of work he does for his job, it wasn’t in his normal work setting or for his employer, so the lost income would not fall under that General Damages coverage.

**Agree.** Shannon’s activity falls within the policy’s definition of “Hot Work” because, while working on her belongings (the motorcycle), she **used/produced a heat source capable of igniting flammable materials** (a lit cigarette), and the resulting fire damage was accidental. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Agree.** Shannon’s welding work on her motorcycle (a belonging) uses a heat source capable of igniting flammable materials, so it qualifies as “Hot Work,” and the resulting accidental fire damage would be covered under that policy definition.

Agree.

Shannon was doing work on her belongings (the motorcycle) using equipment (a gasoline-engine air compressor) that produces heat capable of igniting flammable materials, and the fire resulted from that heat source. That fits the policy’s definition of “Hot Work,” so the loss would be covered under the Hot Work policy.
Agree.
Agree.
Agree.

Disagree.

Denise’s damage occurred while she was moving her belongings herself (using a rented truck), not while they were being removed by **professional removal contractors**, which is required by the policy definition of “House Removal.” Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Disagree.

The policy covers damage “caused while being removed … from the home” by professional movers. Here, the damage occurred during transit after the items were already removed and loaded into the truck, so it likely falls outside the defined “House Removal” coverage.

Disagree

The policy covers damage only when belongings are being removed by **professional removal contractors**. Denise’s uncle, although formerly a professional mover, is helping as a family member and is not acting as a contracted professional removal company for the move.
Disagree.

**Agree.** Harper’s belongings were being stored by a **professional removal contractor** (the moving company) as part of her move, so the flood damage falls within the policy’s “House Removal” coverage.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage **while being stored by professional removal contractors**. Here, Harper asked Bill to store the items **in his personal office**, which looks like an informal/personal arrangement rather than storage provided **in his professional capacity as a removal contractor**. Therefore, the loss likely falls outside the “House Removal” coverage.

**Disagree.**

Even though Jason was at home when the theft occurred, the hackers obtained his Social Security number by infiltrating his **car dealership’s computer network**, meaning the identity theft was **connected with his business**. Because the policy **excludes identity theft connected with the policyholder’s business**, the claim would likely be denied.

**Agree.** The identity theft occurred through Jason’s home network and involved fraudulent personal credit accounts, with no facts indicating it was **connected to his business** as a car salesman, so the business-related exclusion should not apply.

**Disagree.**

The identity theft appears **connected with Jason’s business** because the hackers accessed his **work laptop** (a business device) to obtain his Social Security number. That business connection likely triggers the policy’s exclusion for “identity theft connected with the policyholder’s business,” so coverage would be denied.

**Disagree.**

Even though the fraudulent purchases were made against Jack personally, the identity theft was **connected with his occupation** because the hackers accessed his information by infiltrating the **computer network at his office**. That connection triggers the policy’s exclusion, so the claim would likely be denied.

**Agree.** The identity theft occurred through Jack’s **home** WiFi and involves **fraudulent credit card purchases**, with no facts indicating it was **connected to his occupation** as a business consultant. Therefore, the occupational-connection exclusion likely does not apply, and the claim should be covered.
Disagree.

**Disagree.**

The policy only covers loss or accidental damage occurring **while Vivian is “temporarily away from home.”** Because she **permanently moved abroad**, her absence is not temporary, so the condition for coverage is not met—even though she still owns the house.

Disagree.

The “Loss and Accidental Damage” coverage described applies only to damage that occurs **to the insured property while it is temporarily away from home** (i.e., away-from-home coverage). Vivian’s loss is damage to her **house at home** while she was away, so it doesn’t meet that condition.

Disagree.

The Loss and Accidental Damage coverage applies only when the damage occurs **while the insured property is temporarily away from the home**. Here, the damaged property is the **house itself (the home)**, and it was damaged **at home**, not “away from home.” Also, Vivian’s move abroad appears **not temporary** (indefinite relocation), which further undermines any argument that the policy condition is met.

Final answer is: **Disagree**

Disagree.  

The policy covers key replacement **only in the case of theft**, and Peter **lost** the key (not stolen), so his loss isn’t covered under that provision.

**Agree.** The policy specifically includes **key replacement in the case of theft**, and Peter’s van key was **stolen in a mugging**, so the cost to replace the key should be covered under this policy.

**Disagree.** The policy only covers **key replacement “in the case of theft.”** Here, Peter **voluntarily handed over the van (and its key)** to an acquaintance, and the key was **not returned/possibly lost**, which is not clearly a **theft** as described. Therefore, the key replacement cost would **not be covered** under that provision.

Disagree.

The damage was caused by an accident (the garbage truck toppling over), not by “deliberate destruction or damage” to Olivia’s property, so it doesn’t fit the policy’s definition of Malicious Acts or Vandalism.

Agree

The teenagers’ prank was a deliberate act that caused damage to Olivia’s property (littering/soiling requiring paid cleanup), which fits the policy’s definition of “Malicious Acts or Vandalism” as deliberate destruction or damage.
Disagree.

**Disagree.** The policy covers deliberate damage caused by an **adverse third party** (i.e., a vandal). Beth’s children are not an adverse third party, so this would not fall under “Malicious Acts or Vandalism.”

Agree. The teenagers are adverse third parties, the graffiti is deliberate damage to Beth’s property, and the cost to repaint/repair the wall fits the policy’s definition of covered “Malicious Acts or Vandalism.”

Agree

Beth’s loss fits the policy definition: the graffiti was **deliberate damage** to her property caused by someone other than the policyholder (her son is a **third party**), and given he is estranged and acted intentionally, he can be considered an **adverse** third party. The cost to paint over the graffiti is a covered “repair” cost under the Malicious Acts or Vandalism coverage.
Disagree.
**Agree.** Gwen’s wrist injury was accidental and occurred while she was traveling in her car (driving and parking), so it falls within the policy’s definition of “Personal Accident.”

**Disagree.**  
Because the policy defines “Personal Accident” as an **accidental injury while traveling in your car**, and Gwen’s injury occurred **after she had already parked** (i.e., not while traveling), it likely falls **outside** that defined coverage.

**Disagree.**

Although Penelope was injured while traveling in her car, the policy only covers injuries that **result in a *permanent and total* loss of use of one or more limbs**. Her doctor expects, and she in fact achieves, **full recovery of her knee after three months**, so the loss of use was **temporary**, not permanent. Therefore, she is **not covered** under the Personal Accident definition.

**Disagree.**

Although the injury happened while she was traveling in her car, the policy only covers an accident that results in the **permanent and total loss of use of one or more limbs** (i.e., an arm or a leg). Penelope’s facts describe a permanent loss of function in her **knee**, which is part of a limb but is not itself a “limb,” and it is not clearly a **total loss of use of the entire leg** as the policy requires. Therefore, she would not be covered under this Personal Accident definition.

**Disagree.**

Although Penelope was injured in a covered circumstance (a crash while traveling in her car), the policy pays only if the accident **results in “permanent and total loss of use”** of a limb. Here, the doctor says she **would regain full functionality** with three months of physical therapy. That indicates the loss of use is **not medically permanent as a result of the accident itself**; it becomes permanent only because she does not undergo treatment. In addition, insureds generally must take **reasonable steps to mitigate** their losses rather than allow an avoidable condition to become permanent.

**Final answer: Disagree.**

Disagree.

Although the damage is to third-party property and Dave is legally liable, the policy excludes coverage where the insured “failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the damage from occurring.” Dave knew for months that his brakes were unsafe (warning light on, unresponsive brakes) and did not get them serviced, so the insurer can deny the claim under that exclusion.

**Agree.**

Dave is legally liable for damage to a third party’s property (the restaurant’s landscaping), which fits the policy’s definition of **Public Liability Property Damages**. The exclusion doesn’t apply because Dave **took reasonable steps to prevent the damage**—he regularly checked the manual and had the car serviced for scheduled maintenance, so the brake failure appears unforeseeable rather than due to negligence.

Disagree.

Although the damage is to a third party’s property and Dave is legally liable, the policy excludes coverage if he “failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the damage.” Since Dave knew his car was overdue for scheduled maintenance and still drove it, the insurer can argue he didn’t take reasonable steps to prevent a brake-related accident, so the loss is not covered.

Disagree.

Although wind gusts exceeded 55 mph, the roof damage was caused by Tom dropping the toolbox due to the surprising phone ring, not “damage from wind” as defined by the policy.
Agree.

Disagree.

Although the winds exceeded 55 mph, the immediate cause of the roof damage was the waves (water impact), not the wind itself. Under a policy that covers only “Wind Damage” as defined, this would be characterized as water/wave damage rather than direct wind damage.

Disagree.

The roof damage was caused by a fire that started when Cam dropped a candle, not by the storm itself. The storm (lightning causing a power outage) is at most an indirect, “but-for” link, and the immediate cause of the loss is the accidental fire. Under a policy that covers only “damage caused by a storm,” this kind of intervening cause typically breaks the chain for “storm damage” coverage.
Agree.

Agree.

The lightning storm (a “storm”) set off the chain of events by striking the power line and causing the surge that led to the fire, so the roof damage was “damage caused by a storm” under the policy’s definition.
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Agree.
Agree. Final answer is: **Agree**
Disagree.

Disagree.

The damage described is from earthquake shaking, not from **ground heave of the site** (upward swelling/movement of the ground) or **landslip**. Therefore it would not fall under the policy’s “Ground Heave” coverage as defined.

Disagree.

“Ground heave” refers to upward movement of the ground (or landslip) affecting the building’s site, typically from natural ground movement/soil conditions. Damage caused by vibrations/impact from a nearby vehicle collision is not “ground heave” of the site, so the Ground Heave coverage wouldn’t apply.
Disagree.
**Agree.** Clint’s damage resulted from the ground beneath the house shifting (ground heave), which falls within the policy’s definition of covered “Ground Heave” damage.
Disagree.

Disagree.

The “Trace and Access” coverage applies only to the necessary and reasonable costs of opening up parts of the home to **locate and fix accidental damage to cables or pipes**. Here, Justin’s drywall and floorboard damage was incurred to **retrieve a dropped wedding ring**, not to locate and repair **accidental damage to a pipe or cable** (and the leak repair itself isn’t described as accidental pipe damage). Therefore, this claim would not fall under Trace and Access.

**Agree.** The drywall and floorboard removal are **necessary and reasonable “trace and access” costs** to locate and repair the accidentally damaged pipe, so they fall within the policy’s Trace and Access coverage.

**Agree.** Under the given definition, the damage to the pipe was **accidental**, and the drywall/floorboard removal is a **necessary and reasonable cost to access and fix** the damaged pipe, so it falls within “Trace and Access” coverage.

Disagree.

The Trace and Access cover applies to costs of locating and fixing **accidental damage** to pipes/cables/drains/tanks. Here, the problem was caused by **Jett’s poor installation/workmanship**, not accidental damage, so it would not fall within the Trace and Access coverage.

**Agree.** The policy covers the necessary and reasonable costs to locate and access (including removing floorboards) and fix **accidental damage to pipes** providing services to the home, and the frozen pipes that cracked fit that description.

Disagree.

The Trace and Access benefit applies to locating and fixing **accidental damage** to service pipes. Here, the pipes cracked **gradually due to long-term wear and tear**, not an accident, so the loss generally wouldn’t trigger Trace and Access coverage.

**Disagree.**

At the time the damage occurred, the car was under the **custody/control of Jenny’s friend**, who had Jenny’s **permission** to possess/use it and is **not covered under the policy**. That situation fits the policy’s exclusion, so the Vehicle Damage coverage would not apply.

**Agree.** The exclusion only applies if, at the time of the damage, the car was in the custody or control of someone with Jenny’s permission who is not covered under the policy. Here, the car was parked and not under anyone’s custody or control, so the exclusion doesn’t apply and the Vehicle Damage coverage should cover the loss.

Disagree.

Because Jenny left the car key with her neighbor (someone not covered under the policy) with permission, the insurer can treat the car as being under that person’s “custody or control” at the time of the incident. That triggers the exclusion for Vehicle Damage coverage, so the damage would not be covered under this policy provision.
Disagree.
Agree.

Agree.

The damage (both the misalignment and the resulting scraping) was caused by the accident, not by ordinary “wear and tear” from normal use over time. The wear-and-tear exclusion generally targets gradual deterioration, not accident-triggered damage.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Damage” coverage applies to (1) the car itself or (2) car accessories while in or on the car. A guitar is personal property, not a car accessory, so it isn’t covered under that definition. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Agree.** The GPS navigation system qualifies as a **car accessory**, and since it was **in the car at the time of the accident**, damage to it falls within the policy’s definition of “Vehicle Damage.”

Disagree.

A tablet computer is generally considered personal property, not a “car accessory” (even if used for navigation) unless it is an accessory designed for and installed as part of the vehicle. Therefore, it would not fall under “Vehicle Damage” as defined.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Damage” coverage applies only to (1) damage to Chris’s car or (2) damage to audio equipment while in the car. A work laptop is neither, so it isn’t covered under this policy.
Agree — the policy covers “audio equipment (while in the car),” and Chris’s stereo system and speakers qualify, so the damage is covered under Vehicle Damage.
**Agree.** A portable Bluetooth speaker is “audio equipment,” and it was **in the car** at the time of the accident, so it falls within the policy’s defined “Vehicle Damage” coverage.

Disagree.

The policy covers **“Vehicle Damage,” defined as damage** to the car or to car accessories (including a child car seat while in or on the car). James’s car seat was **stolen**, which is a **theft/loss**, not “damage” as defined by the policy.

**Agree.** The policy covers damage to “car accessories, including children’s car seats (while in or on the car).” Since the car seat was placed **on the trunk (on the car)** when the crash occurred, it falls within the covered definition of Vehicle Damage.

Agree.

The policy covers damage to “car accessories, including children’s car seats (while in or on the car).” At the time of the accident, the car seat was on the car (on the trunk), so it falls within the definition of “Vehicle Damage” coverage.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Theft” coverage is defined as theft-related loss or damage **to the policy holder’s car or to audio equipment in/on the car**. Here, the thief stole **a briefcase containing a work laptop**, which is neither the car itself nor audio equipment. Therefore, this loss is **not covered** under the stated Vehicle Theft policy definition.

Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Agree.**  

The policy covers loss or damage resulting from theft of the policy holder’s **audio equipment while in or on the car**. Since the stereo was stolen from inside Gisele’s car (even though it was parked in her home garage), this fits the definition of **Vehicle Theft** coverage.  

**Final answer: Agree.**

**Agree.** The policy’s “Vehicle Theft” coverage includes theft of **audio equipment while in or on the car**, and Gisele’s wireless Bluetooth headset qualifies as audio equipment that was stolen from inside her car.

**Disagree.**

The “Vehicle Fire” coverage applies to **loss or damage from fire to the car itself or to audio equipment (while in/on the car)**. Gene’s claim is for **burned bills (personal property)**, which are **not** the car or covered audio equipment, so that loss is **not covered** under the described policy.

Agree.

The policy covers “loss or damage as a result of fire” to the car or its audio equipment while in/on the car. The fire (even though it started in the smartphone) spread and caused fire damage to the car’s stereo speaker (audio equipment) inside the car, so that damage falls within the Vehicle Fire coverage.

**Agree.** The policy covers loss or damage **resulting from fire** to Gene’s **car** and to **audio equipment while in or on the car**. Here, a fire occurred in the car and damaged the vehicle and the portable Bluetooth speaker (audio equipment) that was in the car, so the loss falls within the “Vehicle Fire” coverage.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers loss or damage “as a result of **fire**, lightning or explosion.” Here, the cigarette butt **did not ignite a fire**; it only produced smoke that damaged the interior. Because the damage was not the result of a covered peril (fire/lightning/explosion), it would typically **not** fall under “Vehicle Fire” coverage.

Agree. Ken’s car was destroyed as a result of **fire**, which falls squarely within the policy’s “Vehicle Fire” definition (“loss or damage … as a result of fire, lightning or explosion”), regardless of where the fire originated.
**Agree.** The car sustained damage (smoke damage to the interior) **as a result of a fire**, even though the fire started in the smartphone and did not spread to the rest of the vehicle.

**Disagree.**

The “Vehicle Theft” coverage applies to theft-related loss or damage to **the car itself** or **car accessories (while in or on the car)**. The stolen item here was **cash**, which is neither the car nor a car accessory, so this loss is **not covered** under that definition.

Agree.

The seat covers are “car accessories,” and the surveillance footage shows they were stolen while in/on Hunter’s car, which fits the policy’s “Vehicle Theft” definition.

Disagree.

The policy covers theft of the car itself or *car accessories* (items that are part of or attached to the vehicle). A smartphone is generally considered personal property, not a car accessory, even if it’s used for navigation or music while driving. Therefore, the theft of the smartphone from the car would not be covered under this “Vehicle Theft” coverage.

**Disagree.** Dillon is **not** covered because the policy expressly excludes theft losses when the car is taken by an **employee or ex-employee**, and his personal assistant qualifies as an employee. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Agree.** The exclusion only applies if the car was taken by a **family/household member** or an **employee/ex-employee**. The footage shows an **unfamiliar stranger** stealing the car, so the theft falls within the policy’s Vehicle Theft coverage.  

**Final answer is: Agree**

Disagree.

Because Dillon’s estranged wife is still his spouse (and therefore a “member of [his] family”), the policy’s Vehicle Theft exclusion for a car “taken by a member of your family” applies, so the theft would not be covered.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Theft” coverage applies only if Cooper’s car contains a working GPS tracking device. Since Cooper’s car did not have one, the condition for coverage isn’t satisfied, so the theft loss is not covered under that provision.

Agree.

The theft coverage applies only if the car contains a **working GPS tracking device**. Cooper keeps a GPS tracking device in his car, and the car was stolen (as shown by surveillance footage). On these facts, the condition for Vehicle Theft coverage is satisfied, so Cooper is covered by the policy.

Disagree.

Even though Cooper’s smartphone has GPS and can be “tracked,” the policy condition is that **the car contains a working GPS tracking device**—which is typically understood as a **vehicle-installed or vehicle-dedicated tracker** (designed to track the car itself), not a removable personal phone kept in the car for music/navigation. Since the coverage applies *only if* that condition is met, Cooper likely is **not covered** under the Vehicle Theft provision.

**Disagree.** The policy covers **“broken glass”** in the windshield or windows, and Ahmad’s windshield is **not broken**—it only has a minor cosmetic scratch—so it does not fall within the stated coverage.
**Agree.** The damage is **broken glass in the car’s windshield**, which fits the policy’s definition of “Vehicle Glass” damage, so Ahmad’s claim should be covered under that coverage.

Agree. A chipped windshield indicates the glass has been physically damaged (a piece has broken off), which fits the policy definition of “broken glass in the car’s windows or windshield,” even if the windshield is still largely intact and functional.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Glass” coverage applies only to **broken glass** and **scratching to the bodywork caused solely and directly by broken glass**. Here, the damage was caused by a **baseball striking the hood**, not by broken glass (and no glass damage is described), so it falls outside the policy definition.

**Agree.** Aneesh’s windshield damage is “broken glass,” and the scratches to the bodywork were caused solely and directly by the broken glass shards, so both fall within the Vehicle Glass definition.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers **broken glass** and **scratching to the bodywork caused solely and directly by broken glass**. While the **scratches from the mirror shards** would fit that definition (and the **glass portion** of the mirror might), the **side mirror being “severely damaged” by the baseball impact** includes non-glass components (housing/mount/electronics) damaged by the baseball—not by broken glass—so that part is **not covered** under this Vehicle Glass definition.

**Disagree.**

The damage was caused when first responders **forced entry** “because of an emergency or **perceived emergency** involving the policyholder.” Even though there was no real emergency (just dogs), the police forced entry in response to what they reasonably **perceived as an emergency involving Joanne** based on her frantic call, so it fits the definition of “Emergency Damages.”
Disagree.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage caused when first responders **force entry because of an emergency or a perceived emergency involving Joanne**. Here, the police forced entry in response to what was believed to be a burglary (a perceived emergency). The fact that the emergency turned out to be mistaken doesn’t negate that it was a **perceived emergency** prompting forced entry.

**Agree.**  

The policy covers damage to contents caused when **first responders** force entry due to an emergency involving Salma. Here, the forced entry was made by **Sam (a friend/accountant)**, not a first responder (e.g., police, fire, EMS). So the damage from Sam’s forced entry does **not** fall within the definition of “Emergency Damages.”  

**Final answer: Agree**

**Agree.**

The “Emergency Damages” coverage applies only to **loss or damage to the contents** of the home caused by first responders forcing entry due to an emergency involving Salma. Salma’s claim is for **damage caused by the forced entry itself** (typically damage to the door/lock/structure), which is **not “contents”** damage, so it falls outside this defined coverage.

**Agree.** The policy covers damage caused when **first responders** force entry due to an emergency involving the policyholder.
